children.value: Back to the motor from Part 1. The accelerometer streams 1,000 samples per second, and your threshold rule fires a
whenever a sample exceeds +2 g. On a healthy machine, calibration gave you

children.value: You decide to summarise the stream one second at a time:
s show up in each 1,000-sample window.
That count is the alarm metric the operator sees.
How much can the count drift from one second to the next without anything actually being wrong?
If you see a count of 20 in one window, is that worrying — or just normal variation?

children.value: . The distribution is heavily skewed and concentrated near small counts. That regime —
— is exactly where the Binomial morphs into the Poisson distribution (Part 3).
Simulating many 1-second windows from the same machine is the empirical version of the formula. Each simulated window is one independent set of

children.html: nnn

children.value: Why simulate when we have the formula?

Simulation validates the theory and builds intuition. If simulation and formula disagree, one of them is wrong — a powerful debugging technique that survives all the way into deep learning, where closed-form answers stop existing and Monte Carlo is your only tool.
the simulation histogram should hug the theoretical
. Small residual differences shrink as the number of simulated windows increases (also a
